title: Event Safety & Operations Assessment
description: Assessment covering event safety planning, crowd dynamics, weather protocols, and emergency procedures.

questions.question: At what crowd density does significant crush risk become a concern?
questions.explanation: Crowd density above 4 persons/m² creates significant crush risk and limits crowd mobility. At this density, individuals cannot move freely and crowd dynamics can become dangerous.

questions.question: What is the first action when lightning is detected within 10 miles of an outdoor event?
options: Alert the audience to seek shelter
questions.explanation: Lightning within 10 miles is the threshold for immediate suspension of outdoor operations. The 30/30 rule (suspend when thunder follows lightning by <30 seconds, resume 30 min after last thunder) is commonly used.

questions.question: What is the first aid priority for a suspected laser eye injury?
options: Do NOT rub the eye — cover with sterile dressing and seek immediate medical care
Apply cold compress to reduce swelling
questions.explanation: For laser eye injury, the eye should NOT be rubbed (which could worsen retinal damage). Cover with a sterile dressing and immediately transport to emergency ophthalmology care.

questions.question: Which document defines chain of command for safety decisions during an event?
questions.explanation: The Event Safety Plan should clearly define the chain of command for all safety decisions, including who has authority to suspend or stop the show, and escalation procedures.

questions.question: What minimum general liability insurance coverage is recommended for professional laser operations?
questions.explanation: Professional laser operations should carry minimum $1 million per occurrence general liability insurance. Many venues require this as a minimum, and insurers increasingly require evidence of safety training compliance.

questions.question: When should event safety planning ideally begin?
questions.explanation: Safety planning must begin months before the event to allow proper venue assessment, regulatory permit applications (which can take weeks or months), insurance procurement, and staff training.
